systems.name: Mega Drive
systems.summary: Sega's 16-bit console line, known for fast arcade-style ports and a strong action catalog.

systems.name: Master System
systems.summary: Sega's 8-bit console family with strong arcade conversions and a long afterlife in Europe and Brazil.

systems.name: Dreamcast
systems.summary: Sega's final console, remembered for inventive arcade design, early online play, and a cult-classic library.

systems.name: Sega Saturn
systems.summary: Sega's 32-bit console with a rich Japanese library, strong 2D action, and a distinctive late-arcade identity.

systems.name: Nintendo Entertainment System
systems.summary: Nintendo's breakthrough 8-bit console and one of the foundational system libraries in videogame history.

systems.name: Super Nintendo Entertainment System
systems.summary: Nintendo's 16-bit console with a standout library of platformers, role-playing games, and first-party adventure titles.

systems.name: Game Boy
systems.summary: Nintendo's monochrome handheld line and one of the clearest examples of a system-first game catalog.

systems.name: Game Boy Color
systems.summary: Nintendo's color revision of the Game Boy line, bridging classic handheld design and a more modern portable library.

systems.name: Game Boy Advance
systems.summary: Nintendo's 32-bit handheld line with a broad library of platformers, RPGs, remakes, and portable experimentation.

systems.name: Nintendo 64
systems.summary: Nintendo's cartridge-based 3D console, defined by multiplayer landmarks and a concentrated first-party catalog.

systems.name: Nintendo GameCube
systems.summary: Nintendo's compact optical-disc console with a polished first-party lineup and a strong late-arcade-era sensibility.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation
systems.summary: Sony's first console and a major pivot point in the move toward CD-based 3D game publishing.

systems.name: PlayStation Portable
systems.summary: Sony's multimedia-focused handheld with a deep action, RPG, and portable console-style library.
